behaviors.description: Generated reply references specific points from the incoming email rather than producing a generic response.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is whether the real LLM output actually references specific points from the email thread. A mock LLM or unit test can verify that the system *tries* to pass the email to the LLM, but cannot detect if the real LLM ignores the prompt and produces generic output. This requires a real LLM call with real email threads to verify the prompt engineering works as intended.
behaviors.test_description: Using a real LLM, provide an email thread containing specific context (sender name, specific question asked, a unique detail). Generate a draft reply. Verify the draft explicitly references at least 3 specific elements from the email (not just generic salutations or generic email conventions like 'Thank you for reaching out').
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires a real LLM call to verify prompt engineering. Mocks would hide failures where the real LLM ignores instructions to be specific.

behaviors.description: The factual consistency check flags claims in the draft that contradict entries in the knowledge base.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is that the consistency checker fails to detect contradictions that should be caught. This can be tested with a mock KB and real (or mocked) draft text, plus the actual fact-checking logic. The test can create a known-fact scenario (e.g., 'Product X costs $100') and a contradictory claim ('Product X costs $150'), then verify the checker flags the conflict. If the fact-checker is deterministic (semantic matching on embeddings, rule-based logic), Integration is sufficient. However, if fact-checking uses real-time LLM calls for semantic comparison, Agentic would be required; the 40% probability reflects this uncertainty.
behaviors.test_description: Create a mock knowledge base with a specific fact (e.g., 'Service Y was launched in 2022'). Create a draft containing a contradictory claim ('Service Y started in 2021'). Run the consistency check. Assert that the contradiction is flagged with appropriate details.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration test can verify deterministic fact-checking. If the system uses LLM-based fact-checking, an Agentic test would additionally be needed, reflected in the 0.40 Agentic probability.

behaviors.description: When the knowledge base has no relevant entries, the draft includes a disclaimer rather than fabricating facts.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is that the LLM ignores the 'no facts available → include disclaimer' instruction and hallucinates facts anyway. The system can be designed two ways: (1) prompt the LLM to include a disclaimer when KB returns nothing, or (2) post-process the draft to append a disclaimer if KB is empty. Approach (2) is deterministic (Integration), but approach (1) requires verifying the real LLM respects the instruction—a real LLM call is needed because different runs may vary in whether they follow the instruction. The 20% Integration probability accounts for systems using post-processing instead of prompting.
behaviors.test_description: For a real LLM, mock the KB lookup to return no relevant entries. Generate a draft with the real LLM using a prompt that instructs 'include a disclaimer when no facts are available.' Verify the draft contains an explicit disclaimer (e.g., 'I don't have verified information on this') and doesn't contain unsupported claims or hedging that reads as fabrication.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Requires real LLM to verify it honors the instruction. Mocking the LLM would hide the failure where the real LLM hallucinates despite prompting.

behaviors.description: The approval workflow blocks sending until the user explicitly approves—no draft is sent automatically.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is a logic bug where the system sends without waiting for approval (e.g., code always calls send() after draft generation, or approval check is missing). This can be caught by wiring together the draft generation (mock LLM is fine), the approval workflow, and the email send service (mock is fine for the service itself). The test needs to verify: send is NOT called if user rejects, and send IS called if user approves. This is a control flow failure, not LLM-dependent, so Integration is sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Create an integrated test: (1) Generate a draft (mock the LLM), (2) Simulate user explicitly rejecting approval, verify the send service is NOT called. (3) Repeat the sequence with user approval, verify send IS called with the correct draft content.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration test wires workflow components and mocks external services. This catches the failure at the control-flow level without needing real LLM or network calls.

behaviors.description: The full sequence—read thread, generate draft, check facts, present for approval—works end-to-end for a real email thread.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is that something breaks when all components interact (e.g., real email parsing fails on edge cases, real LLM produces unexpected format, fact-checking pipeline breaks under real data). At minimum, the draft generation step requires a real LLM call (Agentic). If the fact-checking step also uses LLM calls (e.g., entailment model, real-time semantic matching), the sequence becomes a multi-step agentic pipeline (Workflow). Since fact-checking implementation is ambiguous, the probabilities are split: 45% Agentic (if fact-checking is deterministic) and 45% Workflow (if fact-checking uses LLM).
behaviors.test_description: Run the full pipeline with a real email thread and real LLM: (1) Parse the email thread, (2) Generate a draft using the real LLM, (3) Run fact-checking on the draft, (4) Present draft for approval (mock user interaction), (5) Verify draft is only sent after explicit approval. Assert that: the draft addresses specific points from the email, fact-checking logic completes without error, and the approval gate is enforced.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: End-to-end test requires real LLM (Agentic minimum) and exercises the full workflow. If fact-checking is LLM-based, this becomes a Workflow test with multiple agentic steps.
